# Optimizer: Findings

*Generated by `pipeline/optimize.py` (OR-Tools CP-SAT, Google's
open-source solver for decisions bound by hard rules). Full data:
`public/data/optimizer.json`, `data/processed/optimized_headways.parquet`.
Every constraint below is asserted in code (`test_optimize.py`), not
assumed: an automated test fails if it is ever broken.*

## Headline

**Calgary's bus network could save ~5.0M vehicle-km per year, about 10%
of the total distance its buses drive (≈2.5M litres of diesel, ≈6,700 t
CO₂, ≈$4.1M/yr), with modeled ridership impact under 2% and no stop
losing service in any period it has today.** (Conservative scenario;
demand modeled, not measured; see FINDINGS-demand.md.)

| scenario | saved km/yr | % of bus vkm | diesel L/yr | t CO₂/yr | $/yr | ridership impact | cells changed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| conservative | 5.03M | 10.4% | 2.51M | 6,739 | $4.1M | −2.0% | 406/1217 |
| moderate | 6.85M | 14.2% | 3.43M | 9,186 | $5.7M | −3.5% | 519/1217 |
| aggressive | 8.02M | 16.6% | 4.01M | 10,743 | $6.6M | −5.0% | 619/1217 |

## What the optimizer is allowed to do

Choose a longer headway (the gap between one bus and the next) per
(route, period, day-type) from {10, 12, 15, 20, 30, 45, 60} min, subject
to:

1. **Capacity**: modeled peak load per trip (riders on board at the
   busiest point, which concentrates as trips thin out) stays within 55
   seats × policy load factor, how full a bus is allowed to get
   (0.8 / 1.0 / 1.25 by scenario).
2. **Availability**: no stop loses service in any period it has today;
   nothing goes worse than hourly; already-hourly service is untouched;
   periods with <4 trips keep their schedule.
3. **Service standards**: routes now at ≤15 min stay ≤15 min (the
   frequent-network promise, mirroring the Primary Transit Network in
   RouteAhead, Calgary's long-range transit plan), and no wait more than
   doubles.
4. **Ridership budget**: boardings (each time a rider steps on a bus)
   lost to longer waits stay under a system-wide cap (2% / 3.5% / 5% by
   scenario), using a headway elasticity of −0.4 as a linear
   approximation, meaning a 10% longer wait costs about 4% of boardings.
   CP-SAT picks the most fuel-per-lost-rider-efficient set of changes.
5. **Fleet**: headways never shorten, so trips and peak vehicles only
   decrease (asserted).

## Why the guardrails exist (methodological honesty)

The first, capacity-only formulation "saved" 49 to 55% of vehicle-km by
cutting MAX BRT lines (bus rapid transit, Calgary's high-frequency
backbone brand) to hourly: a textbook case of an optimizer exploiting its
model's weakest point (the gravity model under-ranks trunk/BRT demand;
see FINDINGS-demand.md limitations). Adding the frequent-network and
doubling guardrails brought it to ~35%, still implausible: with loads
thin almost everywhere, "cut everything to the policy floor" was the
optimum. The ridership-loss budget is what turns the exercise into a
genuine trade-off, and it is the binding constraint, the limit the
optimizer runs into first, in all three scenarios. The lesson worth
stating in any pitch: **the savings estimate is only as strong as the
constraint set**, and this one is deliberately conservative.

## Where the savings come from

- Overwhelmingly **weekend daytime and weekday off-peak** frequency trims
  on long, thin routes: for example route 23 (19→30 min Sat/Sun), route
  43 (21→30), route 302 (26→45), route 20 (27→45).
- The flagged 27 long/low-frequency routes from the supply analysis
  contribute, but so do mid-frequency crosstowns (routes that cross the
  city without going downtown) whose modeled loads leave headroom even at
  doubled headways.
- Nothing comes from the frequent core on weekdays: the PTN guardrail
  and capacity constraint hold it in place.

## Caveats

- **Demand is modeled** (calibrated gravity; totals anchored to published
  boardings, spatial distribution unvalidated). The ridership-impact
  figure inherits that uncertainty. APC (automatic passenger counter)
  data would firm both up.
- Savings assume trips scale linearly with headway (span, the hours a
  route runs, and routing unchanged) and ignore schedule-blocking
  efficiencies and losses (blocking is how trips chain into one bus's
  workday); a real runcut, the detailed scheduling of buses and drivers,
  would shift the numbers some percent either way.
- Fuel factor 0.5 L/km is fleet-average diesel; Calgary's CNG/BEB share
  (compressed natural gas and battery-electric buses) reduces the litres
  (not the km). Restate before any public claim.
- Elasticity −0.4 is a literature constant (a value taken from published
  transit research), not estimated from Calgary data. It sits within the
  range reported by the two standard references: [TCRP Report 95, ch. 9](https://www.trb.org/publications/tcrp/tcrp_rpt_95c9.pdf)
  (Evans, 2004; Transit Cooperative Research Program, a US/Canadian
  transit research body), whose frequency-elasticity estimates run about
  0.26 to 0.5 depending on method, and [TRL Report 593](https://www.trl.co.uk/uploads/trl/documents/TRL593%20-%20The%20Demand%20for%20Public%20Transport.pdf)
  (Balcombe et al., 2004; Transport Research Laboratory, a UK transport
  research body), whose 27-study average for short-run bus service
  elasticity is 0.38. Neither is Calgary-specific. The scenario spread
  (2 to 5% budget) is the sensitivity treatment, showing how the result
  moves if the assumption is off.
- Coordinated-offset scheduling on overlapping corridors (supply finding
  #3), which spaces two routes' buses evenly on a shared stretch, is not
  yet modeled: it's upside, not risk.
